entities.description: Late-fifteenth-century East Anglian paper miscellany with later Robert Melton additions. Smith's edition selects current catalog articles 1–4 and 6–28 and rearranges them.

physicalDescription.extent: ff. ii + 78 + ii; modern foliation 1–81 includes flyleaves
physicalDescription.format: Composite miscellany in original late-fifteenth-century limp parchment binding
notes: Twelve leaves and one flyleaf are missing; the catalog identifies no literary loss after the missing leaves at ff. 7 and 41.
Current catalog describes 29 articles.

rights.basis: Smith states that she supplied the first line from Sloane MS 513, transferred its marginal cast numerals, and prints Sloane variant readings in the 1886 edition. This United States conclusion is limited to that nineteenth-century published material.
rights.component: Sloane-derived first line, cast numerals, and variant readings published in Smith's 1886 dice-prognostic text
notes: No United States conclusion is asserted for unquoted Sloane wording. United Kingdom status is unresolved pending passage-specific pre-1989 publication analysis; medieval age alone does not defeat the 2039 rule.

next_steps: Seek path-specific repository documentation or rights-holder clarification before assigning an open license to the XML artifact.
notes: Until resolved, repository-artifact XML rights remain unknown.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME root Unlicense apply to inherited XML/data/markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ajd3529.xml?

next_steps: Compare multiple physical copies and consult publisher or bibliographic records for issue/state evidence.
openQuestions.question: Does 'Originally printed for private circulation' identify a distinct earlier issue or state?

next_steps: Retain both interpretations unless new codicological or archival evidence resolves the function.
openQuestions.question: Does the 1 May 1492 receipt in the main scribe's hand record an actual transaction or a draft/model text?

next_steps: Check Smith's working papers or another authorial correction; until then label the emendation as inference.
openQuestions.question: Is Smith's 'bd. i, p. 186' a typographical error for Englische Studien VII, pp. 181–182?

next_steps: Consult the physical manuscript or a foliation history/reproduction to document the crosswalk.
openQuestions.question: How exactly does Kölbing's legacy Cotton f. 89 locator crosswalk to the current catalog's Owayne ff. 91v–95r?

next_steps: Collate both witnesses when images or reproductions are available.
openQuestions.question: Which of Harley 116 ff. 124r–125r and Harley 2251 ff. 168r–168v (Smith's legacy f. 173) supplied Smith's 'variaunce' reading?

next_steps: Audit each nineteenth-century edition or quotation against the exact witness passage and establish whether its dissemination constitutes valid publication or other making-available under the applicable United Kingdom rules.
Until that passage-level history is established, keep the supporting-witness public-domain conclusions limited to evidenced United States scopes and treat United Kingdom status as unresolved.
notes: The UK IPO warns that medieval age alone does not establish public-domain status when exact literary witness text remained unpublished at the end of 1988.
openQuestions.question: Which exact supporting passages in Sloane MS 513, Cotton MS Caligula A II, the Vernon Manuscript, Ashmole 61, Trinity B.11.24, Harley MS 116, and Harley MS 2251 were validly made available to the public before 1989 for United Kingdom copyright-duration purposes?

next_steps: Request a path-specific license statement from the provider if redistribution beyond the stated raw-file reuse permission is contemplated.
openQuestions.question: What formal rights status applies specifically to Medictionary's May 2026 wrapper and formatting?

reviewNotes: The catalog subject is the 1886 edition-defined edited selection, not one medieval work or the whole Yale codex.
No same_as relations are used. Version and provenance directions are qualified by exact comparison evidence.
Yale 'Completely digitized' is bounded to all 167 provider-exposed surfaces, including the native-full spine exception.
Every one of the 56 cached files has explicit target_work_presence; absent supporting objects have no work_portion.
The CME root Unlicense is evidence but does not resolve rights in inherited XML/data/markup.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML is a normalized version of a raw University of Michigan OCR/TEI encoding of Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1886 edition-defined selection from the Book of Brome. The raw U-M package, public U-M item, pinned serialization, and May 2026 Medictionary successor are distinct digital layers. Smith selected current Yale articles 1–4 and 6–28, rearranged them into three topical parts, reused three antecedent publications, and collated several components directly or through nineteenth-century editions. The complete Yale IIIF object carries the underlying selected manuscript contents; Vernon, Ashmole 61, Trinity B.11.24, Cotton Caligula A II, Sloane 513, and their scans carry supporting versions or analogues only. Access and rights are recorded separately for historical text, repository XML, scan carriers, manuscript images, and successor markup.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous and attributed medieval writers, scribes, and documentary compilers
catalogSummary.date: Selected witness texts chiefly 1475–1500, with Robert Melton's additions c. 1499–1508; edited selection published 1886
catalogSummary.region: England, especially East Anglia and the Suffolk–Norfolk border
catalogSummary.title: A Common-place Book of the Fifteenth Century, Containing a Religious Play and Poetry, Legal Forms, and Local Accounts

catalogingSubject.label: A Common-place Book of the Fifteenth Century, Containing a Religious Play and Poetry, Legal Forms, and Local Accounts
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1886 edition-defined composite selection from the Book of Brome: selected medieval literary, devotional, legal, administrative, accounting, and medical components rearranged into three topical parts. It is not one medieval authored work, not the whole Yale codex, and not merely a physical book or scan. Stadolnik's later description of the source codex as a miscellany informs interpretation without replacing the edition's printed title or boundary.

contentParts.label: Part 1: Poetry, including a few puzzles and sayings
notes: Topical container only; its descendants carry the count-once intellectual extent.
contentParts.summary: Smith's first topical part combines verse, a play, prayers, ciphers, lists, and one prose trental direction; it is not the manuscript's physical first block.

contentParts.label: Some Old Puzzles and Sayings
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: One saying stanza, one positions list, five separately headed puzzles, and one Daily Rules stanza.

contentParts.label: Sayings
contentParts.summary: Four-line saying stanza beginning with the hart's love of the wood.

contentParts.label: Different positions
contentParts.summary: A table of conventional positions or lodgings for people and animals.

contentParts.label: Five puzzles
notes: The five puzzle tables count once each; decoded and cipher columns are parallel representations, not ten items.
contentParts.summary: Five separately headed cipher puzzles; the source heading supplies the exact cluster count.

contentParts.label: Daily Rules
contentParts.summary: A nineteen-line conduct stanza.

contentParts.label: Rules for Conduct
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: A six-stanza conduct poem.

contentParts.label: Rules for Conduct
contentParts.summary: The complete six-stanza conduct poem.

contentParts.label: Fortune in Life Told by the Casting of Dice
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: A prognostic poem organized into twenty-four cast outcomes.

contentParts.label: Fortune by Dice
contentParts.summary: One prognostic composition; its twenty-four cast outcomes are nested result streams and are not counted as separate works.

contentParts.label: Fragments by Lydgate
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: Two current Yale catalog articles: an incomplete four-stanza Seven Wise Counsels extract and the short God Amends All poem.

contentParts.label: Seven Wise Counsels (extract)
notes: The four stanzas are one incomplete extract, not four independent works.
contentParts.summary: Four virtue stanzas or fragments labeled Prudencia, Justicia, Temperancia, and Fortitudo.

contentParts.label: God Amends All
contentParts.summary: The short poem beginning 'I stond as styll as ony stoun' and ending 'And caritas ys gon with all.'

contentParts.label: The Catechism of Adrian and Epotys
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English catechetical dialogue in verse.

contentParts.label: Adrian and Epotys
contentParts.summary: One catechetical verse dialogue; editorial comparison streams do not add items.

contentParts.label: Play of Abraham and Isaac
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: The Brome Abraham and Isaac religious play.

contentParts.label: Abraham and Isaac
contentParts.summary: One religious dramatic composition, counted as verse despite its cast list and stage directions.

contentParts.label: The Fifteen Signs before Doomsday
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: An apocalyptic verse sequence.

contentParts.label: Fifteen Signs before Doomsday
contentParts.summary: One verse composition; the fifteen signs and stanza divisions are internal structure.

contentParts.label: St Patrick's Purgatory and the Knight Sir Owen
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: The Brome version of the Sir Owen purgatory narrative.

contentParts.label: St Patrick's Purgatory and Sir Owen
contentParts.summary: One narrative poem; Kölbing/Cotton and Auchinleck comparison streams are not additional selected items.

contentParts.label: The Life of St Margaret
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: The fragmentary Brome life of St Margaret.

contentParts.label: Life of St Margaret (fragment)
contentParts.summary: One incomplete hagiographic verse composition.

contentParts.label: Prayers
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: A short two-paragraph set of instructions for repeated prayers.

contentParts.label: Prayers
contentParts.summary: One prayer-direction cluster; the two paragraphs specify related observances and are not independently titled works.

contentParts.label: Directions for a Trental
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.
contentParts.summary: Robert Melton's prose direction for thirty masses, surrounded by Smith's editorial discussion and quotation.

contentParts.label: Directions for a Trental
contentParts.summary: One prose direction; Smith's quoted comparison verse is editorial apparatus and contributes no item.

contentParts.label: A Carol of the Annunciation
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.

contentParts.label: Carol of the Annunciation
contentParts.summary: One carol.

extent.description: Count-once intellectual extent: 47 components or clusters = 35 prose/list/legal/account items + 12 verse compositions, allocated 20/20/7 across the three topical parts. The 3 parts and 24 DIV2 sections are structural and count zero. Raw XML axes remain distinct: 6 DIV1, 24 DIV2, 25 DIV3, 168 LG, and 2,799 L. Excluding 2 LG/17 L in ARGUMENT streams and 6 LG/14 L nested inside outer LG/L lines as variant-note streams yields 160 main LG and 2,768 main L. LG/L are encoding/layout counts and include outcome groups or account lines, so they do not determine the 47-item intellectual count.

formStatements.description: Edition-defined composite of verse, dramatic verse, prose, lists, tables, legal forms, accounts, and editorial apparatus.

notes: Smith's selection uses current Yale articles 1–4 and 6–28; articles 5 and 29 are not selected.
The count-bearing content_parts sum exactly to 47; all topical parts and edition/encoding section containers have item_count 0.
Stadolnik's 'miscellany' terminology applies to interpretation of the source codex and does not replace the printed preferred title.

openQuestions.question: Does the title-page phrase 'Originally printed for private circulation' refer to a distinct earlier issue or state, and if so how is it related to the 1886 Trübner/Goose issue?

next_steps: Retain the explicit count-bearing descendants and revise only if a documented cross-corpus counting policy supersedes this count-once model.
notes: The present exact total is defensible from source headings and content boundaries; this question records policy comparability, not factual uncertainty in the raw axes.

summaries.value: Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1886 edition rearranges selected contents of the late-fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century Book of Brome into poetry and puzzles, manorial law, and private accounts. Its literary components include conduct verse, a dice prognostic, Adrian and Epotys, the Brome Abraham and Isaac play, the Fifteen Signs, St Patrick's Purgatory, a fragmentary Life of St Margaret, prayers, a trental direction, and an Annunciation carol. Administrative material includes common and tax lists, church duties, twelve paired-language legal model documents, court articles, accounts, a 1504 agreement, and an incomplete jaundice recipe.

The edition has three zero-count topical parts and twenty-four zero-count edition sections. A count-once model recognizes forty-seven intellectual components or clusters: twenty in Part 1, twenty in Part 2, and seven in Part 3. Five separately headed cipher puzzles count as five; twelve numbered legal models count as twelve while their Latin/English streams count once per model; stanza, outcome, table-row, and editorial comparison subdivisions do not add works.

This record describes the edition-defined selection. Yale's current catalog has twenty-nine manuscript articles, of which Smith selected articles 1–4 and 6–28 and rearranged them into twenty-four sections. Neither twenty-seven selected witness articles nor twenty-four DIV2 sections is used as the intellectual-item total; the explicit count-bearing descendants in content_parts reconcile to forty-seven.

Joe Stadolnik argues that 'miscellany' better describes the source codex than 'commonplace book' and distinguishes a professional impersonal compilation from Robert Melton's later personal additions. This later interpretation informs the witness model but does not replace the printed 1886 title or the edition-defined catalog boundary.
